mainText: You will not be able to use very many for any one practice period, because a longer time than usual should be spent with each one. Today’s idea should be applied as follows: First, name the situation: “I am concerned about ______.” Then go over every possible outcome which has occurred to you in that connection and which has caused you concern, referring to each one quite specifically, saying: “I am afraid ______ will happen.” If you are doing the exercises properly, you should have some five or six distressing possibilities available for each situation you use, and quite possibly more. It is much more helpful to cover a few situations thoroughly than to touch on a larger number. As the list of anticipated outcomes for each situation continues, you will probably find some of them, especially those which occur to you toward the end, less acceptable to you. Try, however, to treat them all alike to whatever extent you can. After you have named each outcome of which you are afraid, tell yourself: “That thought is an attack upon myself.” Conclude each practice period by repeating today’s idea once more. “Above all else I want to see.” Today’s idea expresses something stronger than mere determination. It gives vision priority among your desires.
